name: Tree.Blocks

values.name: quantumOfBlocks
values.comment:
Determine the "quantum of indentation." In a well-formed
list of blocks, when the indentation level increases, it increases
by one quantum. When it decreases, it decreases by one or
more quanta.

    > "1\n 2\n  3\n    4" |> fromString |> quantumOfBlocks
      1

    > "1\n  2\n    3\n      4" |> fromString |> quantumOfBlocks
       2

    > "1\n  2\n   3" |> fromString |> quantumOfBlocks

values.name: wellFormed
values.comment:
Determine if a list of blocks is well-formed, meaning that when
the indentation level increases, it increases by one quantum
and no more. The return value is a pair consisting of the
quantum of indentation and True or False, depending on
whethe the list is well-formed.

    > "1\n 2\n 3\n  4\n 5" |> fromString |> wellFormed
      (1, True)

    > "1\n  2\n    3\n     4\n 5" |> fromString |> wellFormed
       (1, False)

    > "1\n  2\n  3\n    4\n5" |> fromString |> wellFormed
      (2, True)

name: Tree.Build
comment:
Tools for building
a tree from a string or a list of blocks. A tree
is represented in text as an outline:

    Example:

        > data = "1\n 2\n 3\n 4\n5\n 6\n 7"

        > fromString "?" identity data
          Tree "0" [
                Tree "1" [Tree "2" [],Tree "3" [], Tree "4" []]
              , Tree "5" [Tree "6" [],Tree "7" []]
              ]

The first argument of fromString is a label for a default node.
The second argument, of type (String -> node), tells how to build a node from a string
representation of that node. Here we use the representation of rose trees of
[elm/rose-tree](https://package.elm-lang.org/packages/zwilias/elm-rosetree/latest/).

@docs fromString, fromBlocks, forestFromString, forestFromBlocks, Error

values.name: forestFromBlocks
values.type: a -> (String.String -> a) -> (a -> String.String) -> List.List Tree.Blocks.Block -> Result.Result Tree.Build.Error (List.List (Tree.Tree a))

values.name: forestFromString
values.comment:
Example:

    > forestFromString "?" identity identity "1\n 2\n 3"
      Ok [Tree "1" [Tree "2" [],Tree "3" []]]

    > forestFromString "?" identity identity "1\n 2\n 3\n4\n 5\n 6"
    Ok [Tree "1" [Tree "2" [],Tree "3" []],Tree "4" [Tree "5" [],Tree "6" []]]
values.type: a -> (String.String -> a) -> (a -> String.String) -> String.String -> Result.Result Tree.Build.Error (List.List (Tree.Tree a))

values.name: fromBlocks
values.type: node -> (String.String -> node) -> List.List Tree.Blocks.Block -> Result.Result Tree.Build.Error (Tree.Tree node)

values.name: fromString
values.type: node -> (String.String -> node) -> String.String -> Result.Result Tree.Build.Error (Tree.Tree node)

name: Tree.Lib
comment:
Utility functions. preOrder and levelOrder transform
Tree a to List a, where the nodes are listed in the
given order. Given Tree a, the edges function returns
List (a,a), where a pair (p,q) is in the returned list
if and only if q is a child of p.

@docs preOrder, levelOrder, edges

values.name: edges
values.comment:
Return a list of pairs (x,y) where

        - x and y are of type a
        - (x,y) is in the list if and only if y is a chiild of x.

name: Tree.Random
comment:
Utilities for generating and collecting statistics on random trees.

@docs generate, generateOutline, depths

values.name: depths
values.comment:
depths k n seed:  generate n random trees of k nodes using the given seed
    and collect statistics on their depths

    > depths 10 10 0
    [(2,1),(3,2),(4,3),(5,2),(8,2)] -- one tree of depth two, two trees of depth three, etc.

    > depths 10 10 1
    [(2,1),(3,1),(4,3),(5,2),(7,1),(8,2)]

    > depths 10 10 2
    [(2,1),(3,2),(4,3),(5,2),(7,1),(8,1)]
values.type: Basics.Int -> Basics.Int -> Basics.Int -> List.List ( Basics.Int, Basics.Int )

values.name: generate
values.comment:
Generate a random rose tree with the given number nodes
and with given seed.

name: Tree.Render
comment:
Functions for transforming a tree or a forest to a string.

@docs toString, forestToString

values.name: forestToString
values.comment:
The first argument is the quantum of indentation. The second
is a function for transforming a node of a forest to a string.

values.name: toString
values.comment:
The first argument is the quantum of indentation. The second
is a function for transforming a node of a tree to a string.

name: Tree.Svg
comment:
Helper functions for rendering trees:

    import Tree.Build
    import Tree.Transform

    tree =
        Tree.Build.fromString "?" identity "1\n 2\n 3\n 4\n"

    graph =
        Result.map (Tree.Transform.toGraph preferences identity) tree |> Result.withDefault []


    Tree.Svg.render (Tree.Svg.transform 280 100 60 60 0.5 graph)

@docs render, transform

values.name: transform
values.type: Basics.Float -> Basics.Float -> Basics.Float -> Basics.Float -> Basics.Float -> Tree.Graph.Graph -> Tree.Graph.Graph

aliases.name: Preferences
aliases.type: { halfAngle : Basics.Float, initialEdgeLength : Basics.Float, scaleFactor : Basics.Float, ballRadius : Basics.Float, ballColor : String.String }

values.name: defaults
values.type: { halfAngle : Basics.Float, initialEdgeLength : number, scaleFactor : Basics.Float, ballRadius : number, ballColor : String.String }

values.name: toGraph
values.comment: Transform a Tree to a Graph: a data structure that can be rendered to SVG.
